title: host-asterinas-migrate: Haiku fan-out import of the markdown tree
body:
# Goal

Migrate the asterinas-workspace markdown tree (~968
issues, ~122 directories) into jjforge using a fan-out
of Haiku agents.

End state: every issue in
`~/p/asterinas-workspace/issues/` is on a jjforge
`issues` bookmark in a clone of the asterinas-workspace
repo; `jjf ready` against it returns sensible answers;
the markdown tree stays in place as a read-only archive.

# Why now

Blocked on `host-asterinas-audit` (`1588625`) and on
whatever feature tickets that audit identifies. Filed
sketched so the shape is in view; concrete scope locks
in once the audit doc lands and the feature tickets are
either shipped or explicitly skipped.

# Approach

Haiku fan-out. Many small parallel agents over disjoint
slices.

Each agent gets:
- A slice of the markdown tree (e.g. one phase's epic,
  or one cross-cutting subdir).
- The audit doc (`docs/host-asterinas-audit.md`) as its
  mapping reference.
- Instructions to read each file, apply the mapping
  rules, and emit `jjf new` calls.

Slices are disjoint by slug-prefix so two agents don't
race on the bookmark. Each Haiku agent claims a slice
via `jjf claim <id> --reason "haiku-slice-X"` or
similar atomic primitive (TBD by audit).

# Scope

## Slicing strategy

Sketched, refined by audit:
- One agent per `phase-N-<theme>/epic-NN-<name>/`
  subdir. ~30 epics in the asterinas tree → ~30
  parallel slices.
- Cross-cutting issues get their own agent.

## Per-agent contract

Each Haiku gets a prompt that says:

- Read every `.md` file under <slice-path>.
- For each file, apply the mapping rules in
  `docs/host-asterinas-audit.md`:
  - Title from the first H1.
  - Slug from the filename (already kebab-case).
  - Type per the audit's status-tag rule.
  - Labels per the audit's tag rules.
  - Status open/closed/blocked/in-progress per the
    audit's `**Status:**` parser.
  - Block reason if applicable (jjf block).
  - Parent-child edge to the phase/epic parent (the
    enclosing directory's README maps to an epic).
- Emit `jjf new` per file. Capture the returned id.
- After the slice is done, write a one-paragraph
  closing comment per slice with: files processed,
  files skipped (and why), follow-up tickets filed
  for ambiguous cases.

## Orchestrator (us)

- File a parent ticket per slice via `jjf new`.
- Dispatch the Haiku per slice, isolated worktree.
- Aggregate the per-slice reports.
- Spot-check N random imports against their source
  markdown.

## Cutover

- The asterinas markdown tree stays in place as a
  read-only archive for 30 days post-migration.
- A note appended to `~/p/asterinas-workspace/issues/README.md`
  pointing operators at `jjf ready`.
- The asterinas-side CLAUDE.md gets an update too.

# Acceptance

- [ ] Every asterinas issue in the tree has a
  corresponding jjforge ticket (modulo explicit skips
  documented in the audit).
- [ ] `jjf ready` in the asterinas clone returns
  sensible answers.
- [ ] The phase → epic → ticket hierarchy is expressed
  via parent-child dep edges.
- [ ] Cross-cutting label-encoding lossless or
  documented losses.
- [ ] A migration report doc at
  `docs/host-asterinas-migration-report.md` lists
  files-processed / skipped / follow-ups per slice.

# Out of scope

- Custom Python or Rust import tooling. We're using
  Haiku agents as the import tooling.
- Editing the source asterinas tree.
- The orchestration thesis (`585b84b`) — that's the
  next epic, kicks in after this one closes.

# Blocked by

- `1588625 host-asterinas-audit` and whatever
  implementation tickets it identifies.
